heb.paragraph: Paul uses two related adjectives in this passage: sarkinos (v. 1, ‘made of flesh’) and sarkikos (v. 3, ‘behaving according to the flesh’). The first (sarkinos) describes the Corinthians’ condition as infant believers; the second (sarkikos) implies culpable behaviour — they are acting according to merely human standards despite having the Spirit. The distinction introduces a third category beyond the pneumatikos/psychikos of chapter 2: the immature believer who possesses the Spirit but lives as though they do not.

refs.note: Jesus uses the same sowing-reaping imagery to describe the collaboration of different workers in God’s harvest field.
The author of Hebrews employs the same milk-versus-solid-food metaphor for spiritual maturity.
Jesus’ parable of the sower uses agricultural imagery for the word’s reception, paralleling Paul’s planting metaphor.

notes.note: Paul’s charge of spiritual immaturity is devastating. He addresses them as ‘mere infants in Christ’ (nēpioi en Christō), unable to receive solid food. The proof of their immaturity is not doctrinal ignorance but behavioural evidence: jealousy (zēlos) and quarrelling (eris) demonstrate they are walking ‘in the manner of mere humans’ (kata anthrōpon).

The slogans ‘I follow Paul’ and ‘I follow Apollos’ are symptomatic of a worldly mindset that evaluates ministers by human standards. Paul and Apollos are not rivals but servants (diakonoi) — the word denotes table waiters, the lowest category of service.

Paul planted, Apollos watered, but God made it grow. The emphasis falls not on the human agents but on God’s causative role. The one who plants and the one who waters are one in purpose (hen eisin), and each will be rewarded according to their own labour. This lays the groundwork for the building metaphor in vv. 10–15.

Calvin observes that Paul does not deny the Corinthians are believers but charges them with arrested development. Spiritual infancy at conversion is natural; spiritual infancy years later is culpable. Calvin draws the lesson that preachers must accommodate their audience’s capacity while pressing them toward maturity.

Calvin notes that the imagery of planting and watering corrects two errors: depreciating ministers (they are God’s co-labourers) and idolising them (God alone gives the growth). The balance between divine sovereignty and human instrumentality is carefully maintained.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The textual variant between sarkinois (‘made of flesh’) and sarkikois (‘fleshly/worldly’) is theologically significant. Sarkinois (better attested) describes their nature as human; sarkikois (v. 3) describes their conduct as sub-Christian. Paul shifts from description to accusation.

The phrase theou gar esmen synergoi (‘for we are co-workers of God’) is grammatically ambiguous: it could mean ‘co-workers belonging to God’ or ‘co-workers with God.’ The genitive theou likely functions as a possessive: Paul and Apollos belong to God, and so does the Corinthian ‘field’ and ‘building.’

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that Paul introduces the sarkinos category not as a permanent third class of Christian but as a rebuke: the Corinthians should be pneumatikoi by now, but their factional behaviour shows they are functioning at the level of the psychikos. The factions are proof of spiritual regression.

Fee notes the shift from agricultural to architectural metaphor at v. 9b, signalling a new development in the argument. The double metaphor (‘God’s field, God’s building’) emphasises divine ownership: the church belongs to God, not to its human leaders.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton argues that the milk/solid-food contrast is not about doctrinal complexity but about the capacity to live by the cross’s logic. ‘Solid food’ is the ability to embrace the cruciform pattern of self-giving that the Corinthians’ competitive factionalism rejects.

Thiselton highlights that diakonoi (‘servants’) is deliberately chosen over apostoloi or other status-bearing titles. Paul deconstructs the Corinthian honour system by insisting that leaders are servants, not patrons.

hist.context: Paul now applies the wisdom-foolishness argument directly to the Corinthian factions. The agricultural imagery of planting and watering (vv. 6–9) draws on common Mediterranean farming practice: one worker plants the seed, another irrigates, but only God provides the growth (auxanō). In first-century Corinth, the surrounding Isthmus was cultivated with grain, olives, and vines, making the imagery vivid and immediate. Paul and Apollos are co-labourers (synergoi), not competitors — a radical reframing of the patron-client model that drove the Corinthian factions.

heb.paragraph: The noun themelios (foundation) in vv. 10–12 carries architectural and theological weight. In the ancient world, the foundation determined a building’s stability and alignment. Paul identifies the foundation as Jesus Christ himself — not a doctrine about Christ, but the person of the crucified and risen Lord. No other foundation can be laid (v. 11). The verb tithemi (to lay/place) implies deliberate, authoritative positioning: Paul as ‘expert builder’ (sophos architektōn) laid this foundation in Corinth.

refs.note: Paul develops the building metaphor further: Christ is the cornerstone, the apostles and prophets the foundation, and believers are being built into a holy temple.
The metaphor of a great house with vessels of various materials (gold, silver, wood, clay) echoes the materials imagery here.
Peter applies the building/stone imagery to Christ as the living stone and believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house.

notes.note: Paul calls himself a ‘wise master builder’ (sophos architektōn), using the term from which English derives ‘architect.’ His role was to lay the foundation — Christ — in Corinth. Others build upon it. The critical point: no one can lay a different foundation. Any teaching that displaces Christ from the centre is building on sand.

The six building materials form two triads: durable (gold, silver, precious stones) and combustible (wood, hay, straw). These represent the quality of ministry — sound biblical teaching versus superficial or erroneous teaching. The ‘Day’ will test everything by fire.

The builder whose work survives receives a reward; the builder whose work burns up suffers loss but is personally saved — ‘only as one escaping through the flames.’ This passage distinguishes between salvation (which is secure) and reward (which depends on faithful service). It is not a text about purgatory but about eschatological evaluation of ministry.

Calvin reads Paul’s self-identification as ‘wise master builder’ not as boasting but as establishing responsibility: those who build after Paul bear accountability for what they construct. Calvin warns teachers who build with ‘wood, hay, straw’ — human speculation, philosophical novelties, or traditions that displace Scripture.

Calvin firmly rejects the Roman Catholic interpretation that this passage teaches purgatory. The fire is eschatological judgment, not post-mortem purification. The one saved ‘as through fire’ is not refined in fire but barely escapes it — saved despite worthless work, not because of purifying suffering.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term sophos architektōn is not ironic but genuinely self-referential: Paul acknowledges his role as the founding church planter. The adjective sophos (‘wise/skilled’) echoes the wisdom vocabulary of chapters 1–2, now applied positively to Paul’s apostolic function.

The phrase hōs dia pyros (‘as through fire’) is a vivid metaphor for a narrow escape. The preposition dia with genitive denotes passage through a threatening medium. The image is of someone fleeing a burning building with nothing but their life.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee stresses that the entire passage concerns church leadership, not individual Christian living. The ‘builders’ are those who teach and lead the community Paul founded. The quality of their work will be assessed eschatologically. Fee warns against universalising this into a general doctrine of rewards and losses for all believers.

Fee argues that ‘saved, but only as one escaping through the flames’ is deliberately shocking imagery: the teacher whose work is burned up retains salvation but has nothing to show for a lifetime of ministry. It is a warning of devastating ministerial failure, not a consoling doctrine of second chances.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton notes that architektōn in the ancient world denoted not merely a designer but the master craftsman who supervised the entire construction process. Paul’s claim is both functional (he founded the church) and authoritative (the foundation he laid is normative).

Thiselton draws on Malachi 3:2–3 (‘a refiner’s fire’) and the Stoic notion of ekpyrōsis (cosmic conflagration) as background to the fire imagery. The eschatological fire is not punitive but revelatory: it discloses the true nature of each builder’s work.

hist.context: Paul shifts from agricultural to architectural metaphor, a transition signalled at v. 9b (‘God’s building’). The imagery of testing by fire (v. 13) may draw on the common ancient Near Eastern and Greco-Roman practice of assaying metals. The ‘Day’ (v. 13) refers to the eschatological day of judgment when all human work will be exposed and evaluated. The materials listed — gold, silver, costly stones versus wood, hay, straw — represent varying qualities of ministry built on the Christ-foundation. This passage does not describe salvation by works but the evaluation of ministerial quality.

heb.paragraph: The noun naos (temple/sanctuary) in v. 16 denotes the inner sanctuary where God’s presence dwells, as distinct from hieron, the broader temple complex. Paul’s assertion ‘you are God’s temple’ uses the plural ‘you’ (hymeis), making the temple the community, not the individual (contrast 6:19 where the individual body is the temple). In a city dominated by pagan temples — including the famous temple of Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth — Paul’s claim is provocative: the Christian assembly, not any stone structure, is the dwelling place of the divine Spirit.

refs.note: Paul restates the temple imagery: ‘We are the temple of the living God,’ citing Leviticus 26:11–12 and Ezekiel 37:27.
The community as a growing temple in which God dwells by his Spirit — the ecclesiological extension of the same metaphor.
Paul quotes Eliphaz’s words (here applied positively): God catches the wise in their craftiness — a rare Pauline use of Job.

notes.note: This is a corporate warning, not an individual health text. The temple is the church community; the destroyer is the false teacher who corrupts it through divisive wisdom. Paul’s warning is severe: God will destroy the one who destroys his temple. The severity reflects the sanctity of the community as the Spirit’s dwelling place.

Paul issues a direct command: ‘Do not deceive yourselves.’ If anyone among them thinks they are wise by worldly standards, they must become a ‘fool’ by embracing the cross’s logic. The quotations from Job and the Psalms close the case: all human wisdom is futile before God.

The climactic reversal: ‘all things are yours.’ Paul, Apollos, Cephas, the world, life, death, the present, the future — all belong to the church. But the church belongs to Christ, and Christ to God. This hierarchy simultaneously elevates believers (they possess everything) and humbles them (they belong to Christ).

Calvin emphasises that this passage warns teachers and leaders, not ordinary believers, about the consequences of corrupting the church. The severity of the penalty — destruction — matches the gravity of the crime: defiling the temple where God’s Spirit dwells.

Calvin observes that the Corinthians had inverted the relationship between leaders and people. Instead of leaders serving the church, church members were serving leaders through factional loyalty. Paul restores the proper order: ministers serve the church, the church serves Christ, and Christ serves the Father’s glory.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The question ouk oidate (‘don’t you know?’) appears ten times in 1 Corinthians and consistently introduces truths the Corinthians should know but are failing to practice. It functions as a gentle rebuke.

Paul’s quotation of Job 5:13 is the only NT citation from the book of Job. Significantly, the words are spoken by Eliphaz — one of Job’s friends whose theology is partially wrong — yet Paul applies them validly to divine wisdom’s superiority over human cunning.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that the destruction warning is directed at specific individuals in Corinth — probably the leaders of the factions — who are tearing the community apart. The Spirit’s dwelling in the community means that divisive behaviour is an assault on God’s own presence.

Fee reads ‘all things are yours’ as the positive corollary of the entire argument: because the Corinthians belong to Christ, they need not compete for status through human leaders. The eschatological sweep (‘life, death, present, future’) expands the argument beyond ecclesiology to cosmology.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton stresses the corporate dimension: naos theou este (‘you are God’s temple’) uses the second person plural. The temple is the community, not the individual believer. This corrects individualistic misreadings that detach the text from its ecclesiological context.

Thiselton identifies the rhetorical strategy as a surprising reversal (peripeteia): the Corinthians thought they were choosing between leaders; Paul reveals that all leaders already belong to them. The sequence panta hymōn — hymeis de Christou — Christos de theou establishes a descending hierarchy of ownership that terminates in God.

hist.context: Paul draws the argument to its climactic application. The Corinthians are not merely God’s field or building but God’s temple (naos) — the dwelling place of the Holy Spirit. Anyone who ‘destroys’ (phtheirei) this temple through divisive teaching will themselves be destroyed (phtherei). The wordplay is deliberate: the verb phtheirō encompasses both moral corruption and physical destruction. Paul then returns to the wisdom theme, quoting Job 5:13 and Psalm 94:11 to demonstrate that all human wisdom — including the wisdom of Paul, Apollos, and Cephas — is subordinate to God. The climactic declaration ‘all things are yours’ (v. 21) reverses the factionalism: instead of belonging to leaders, all leaders belong to the church, and the church belongs to Christ.

themes.note: Judgment and Holiness dominate: the fire-test of vv. 12–15 and the temple-destruction warning of vv. 16–17 constitute some of the most severe eschatological warnings in the Pauline corpus. Sovereignty remains strong as Paul insists that growth belongs to God, not human agents.

rows.label: vv.1–9
rows.text: Spiritual Infancy and the Agricultural Metaphor

rows.label: vv.10–15
rows.text: The Foundation and the Test of Fire

rows.label: vv.16–23
rows.text: The Temple of God and the Reversal of Ownership

lit.note: Chapter 3 shifts from theological argument (chs. 1–2) to direct application: the Corinthians’ factionalism proves their immaturity. Three metaphors — field, building, temple — progressively intensify the argument, culminating in the paradoxical claim that all things belong to the church because the church belongs to Christ.

hebtext.note: Paul uses two related adjectives in this passage: sarkinos (v. 1, ‘made of flesh’) and sarkikos (v. 3, ‘behaving according to the flesh’). The first (sarkinos) describes the Corinthians’ condition as infant believers; the second (sarkikos) implies culpable behaviour — they are acting according to merely human standards despite having the Spirit. The distinction introduces a third category beyond the pneumatikos/psychikos of chapter 2: the immature believer who possesses the Spirit but lives as though they do not.

The noun themelios (foundation) in vv. 10–12 carries architectural and theological weight. In the ancient world, the foundation determined a building’s stability and alignment. Paul identifies the foundation as Jesus Christ himself — not a doctrine about Christ, but the person of the crucified and risen Lord. No other foundation can be laid (v. 11). The verb tithemi (to lay/place) implies deliberate, authoritative positioning: Paul as ‘expert builder’ (sophos architektōn) laid this foundation in Corinth.

The noun naos (temple/sanctuary) in v. 16 denotes the inner sanctuary where God’s presence dwells, as distinct from hieron, the broader temple complex. Paul’s assertion ‘you are God’s temple’ uses the plural ‘you’ (hymeis), making the temple the community, not the individual (contrast 6:19 where the individual body is the temple). In a city dominated by pagan temples — including the famous temple of Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth — Paul’s claim is provocative: the Christian assembly, not any stone structure, is the dwelling place of the divine Spirit.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Apollos
ppl.role: Eloquent Alexandrian teacher; discipled by Priscilla and Aquila
ppl.text: Apollos is an Alexandrian Jew who arrives at Ephesus knowing the way of God accurately but only knowing John’s baptism (Acts 18:24–25). After Priscilla and Aquila complete his instruction, he becomes a powerful apologist in Achaia, "vigorously refuting his Jewish opponents in public debate" (Acts 18:28). Paul’s Corinthian correspondence shows Apollos became an unintended source of factionalism (1 Cor 1:12), though Paul regards him as a fellow worker.

ppl.name: Cephas
ppl.text: Paul then returns to the wisdom theme, quoting Job 5:13 and Psalm 94:11 to demonstrate that all human wisdom — including the wisdom of Paul, Apollos, and Cephas — is subordinate to God…

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: He addresses them as ‘mere infants in Christ’ (nēpioi en Christō), unable to receive solid food…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Foundation and the Temple

translations.text: What, after all, is Apollos? And what is Paul? Only servants, through whom you came to believe—as the Lord has assigned to each his task.
What then is Apollos? What is Paul? Servants through whom you believed, as the Lord assigned to each.
Brothers and sisters, I could not address you as people who live by the Spirit but as people who are still worldly—mere infants in Christ.
But I, brothers, could not address you as spiritual people, but as people of the flesh, as infants in Christ.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul’s charge of spiritual immaturity is devastating. He addresses them as ‘mere infants in Christ’ (nēpioi en Christō), unable to receive solid food. The proof of their immaturity is not doctrinal ig…"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin observes that Paul does not deny the Corinthians are believers but charges them with arrested development. Spiritual infancy at conversion is natural; spiritual infancy years later is culpable.…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The textual variant between sarkinois (‘made of flesh’) and sarkikois (‘fleshly/worldly’) is theologically significant. Sarkinois (better attested) describes their nature as human; sarkikois (v. 3) de…"

debate: The question ouk oidate (‘don’t you know?’) appears ten times in 1 Corinthians and consistently introduces truths the Corinthians should know but are …
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Foundation and the Temple within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Jesus uses the same sowing-reaping imagery to describe the collaboration of different workers in God’s harvest field.
The author of Hebrews employs the same milk-versus-solid-food metaphor for spiritual maturity.
Jesus’ parable of the sower uses agricultural imagery for the word’s reception, paralleling Paul’s planting metaphor.
Paul develops the building metaphor further: Christ is the cornerstone, the apostles and prophets the foundation, and believers are being built into a holy temple.
The metaphor of a great house with vessels of various materials (gold, silver, wood, clay) echoes the materials imagery here.
Peter applies the building/stone imagery to Christ as the living stone and believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house.

discourse.thesis: The Corinthians' factions reveal spiritual immaturity — the builders of God's community will be judged by the quality of their work, and all human boasting in leaders misunderstands that leaders are merely God's servants.

nodes.text: The spiritual immaturity diagnosis: Paul could not address them as spiritual (πνευματικοῖς) but as people of the flesh (σαρκίνοις), infants in Christ. He gave them milk, not solid food. Even now they are still not ready — there is jealousy and strife among you. Walking according to the flesh is proved by the faction-loyalty: 'I follow Paul... I follow Apollos.'

What then is Apollos? What is Paul? Servants (διάκονοι) through whom you believed — as the Lord assigned to each. Paul planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth. The planter and waterer are nothing — God who gives growth is everything. Each will receive wages according to their labor. We are God's fellow workers; you are God's field, God's building.

children.text: The planter and waterer are one (ἕν εἰσιν) — they share a common identity as servants. The faction model (I follow Paul, I follow Apollos) imposes a rivalry on people who see themselves as teammates. The Corinthians' divisions are not merely sociological but theologically incoherent.

nodes.text: Building metaphor: Paul laid the foundation (Christ Jesus) as a skilled master builder. Others build on it. Each is to take care how they build — gold, silver, precious stones or wood, hay, straw. Each one's work will be revealed by fire — tested, though the builder may escape 'as through fire.' You are God's temple (ναός) — the Spirit dwells in you. If anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy him.

children.text: The temple identity — 'you are God's temple' — is plural: the community together is the dwelling place of the Spirit. Division is therefore a form of temple desecration. The warning is serious: God will destroy those who destroy his temple.

nodes.text: Let no one deceive himself — if anyone thinks he is wise in this age, let him become a fool so he may become wise. The wisdom of this world is folly with God. Therefore let no one boast in men — for all things are yours, whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas or the world or life or death or the present or the future — all are yours, and you are Christ's, and Christ is God's.

children.text: The ironic reversal: the Corinthians say 'I belong to Paul/Apollos/Cephas.' Paul says: Paul/Apollos/Cephas belong to YOU. And you belong to Christ. The leaders are gifts given to the whole community, not competing loyalties demanding allegiance.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 3 uses three overlapping metaphors for the church — field (v. 9), building (vv. 10-15), and temple (vv. 16-17) — all pointing to the same conclusion: the church is God's, not the leaders'. The agricultural metaphor establishes servant-identity; the building metaphor establishes accountability; the temple metaphor establishes holiness.

coaching.tip: 'I, brothers, could not address you as spiritual people, but as people of the flesh' (v. 1). The Corinthians thought they were spiritual elite; Paul calls them infants. Evidence: 'jealousy and strife' (v. 3). Factions around leaders — Paul, Apollos, Cephas — prove immaturity. Spiritual maturity is measured by unity, not by favorite teacher.

'Each one's work will become manifest... the fire will test what sort of work each one has done' (vv. 13-14). Building metaphor: gold, silver, precious stones survive fire; wood, hay, stubble burn. The foundation (Christ) is fixed; the building materials vary. Ministry quality will be revealed. Some saved 'as through fire' (v. 15) — barely.
